content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Can we talk about "The Uncanny Valley" of Scripted Dialogue? Looking for shows/movies where people actually speak like humans.

**Score:** 146
**Body:** I’m on a hunt for media that breaks away from the "Standard Screenwriter" trap. You know the one: where characters are unnaturally articulate, every line perfectly follows the last, and nobody ever stumbles over a thought.

I want to find shows or movies (from any country) that capture the mechanics of actual human interaction. In real life, we don't speak in monologues. We speak in fragments, grunts, and unfinished ideas.

I'm looking for recommendations that master these specific "Real-World" traits:

* The Messy Overlap: I want to see scenes where three people are talking at once because they’re excited, or where someone gets cut off mid-sentence and doesn't get to finish their "deep" point.
* Non-Verbal Heavy Lifting: Shows where a character’s entire reaction to a major life event is just a sharp intake of breath, a "pfft," or a shrug, rather than a 2-minute speech about their feelings.
* Micro-Expressions & Mannerisms: I want to see characters who have specific, grounded physical tics or ways of reacting that feel tied to where they grew up, not just "Actor-School" expressions.
* The "Umm/Ahh/Ohh" Factor: Give me the filler words. Give me the stuttering when someone is nervous. Give me the awkward "dead air" where characters are just staring at each other because they genuinely don't know what to say.
* Hyper-Specific Shorthand: I love when a show doesn't explain its world to me. If two best friends are talking, they should use inside jokes and half-words that I, the audience, have to catch up on.

What is the one show or movie that made you feel like you were eavesdropping on real people rather than watching a production? I don't care about the genre, could be sci-fi, horror, or a kitchen-sink drama, as long as the dialogue feels "raw."



### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I've got one for you. All the President's Men. It's literally something I've mentioned to people before about how they talk. They speculate, struggle to find the right words. Robert Redford (playing Bob Woodward) even gets confused juggling phone calls and refers to somebody with the wrong name.
  - 70s cinema is really good at this in general 
2. Before Sunrise, Before Sunset, An Affair to Remember, The Goonies, His Girl Friday, 12 Angry Men

I wish I could remember whatever I watched recently that had so much overlapping dialogue I was impressed.

EDIT: I REMEMBERED-- surprisingly enough Pratical Magic. I think they did it because it adds to the whimsy of the Owens women.
3. Director Robert Altman was pretty famous for overlapping dialog and generally creating scenes where you feel like you’re dropping in on something that was going on before you got there and will continue after you’ve gone. So his works might be the sort of thing you’re trying to find.

If you don’t know him, I’d recommend [McCabe & Mrs. Miller (1971)](https://www.rogerebert.com/reviews/great-movie-mccabe-and-mrs-miller-1971) and [The Player (1992)](https://www.rogerebert.com/reviews/the-player-1992) as good places to start.

I don’t remember to what degree these films show the specific things you mention, but then again, that’s the point, isn’t it?: if it’s done right, you don’t notice it, because it feel like real life.
  - This is one of the things I love about **Gosford Park**. There’s something crazy like 30 speaking parts or something, and even the people who have just a handful of lines feel like fully realized characters. It may seem like “fussy articulate British people” dialogue, but there’s a lot of “not flashy” dialogue where you just have to keep pace with why an off-hand comment about someone’s dress is really an insult that reveals deep-seated character tensions and stuff.
4. Anatomy of a Fall—I could not stop thinking about how this film captured real conversation and fights.
5. John Cassavetes' movies strike me that way.  
6. The show Somebody Somewhere is this. It feels like real people having wonderful, awkward, terrible conversations.

Jaws does the overlapping talking, real feel convos too.
7. If you want under scripted relaism, check out Steven Soderbergh's Bubble. He cast non-actors, and gave them scene objectives instead of a script. It really feels like your a fly on the wall. 
8. A Scanner Darkly

## 2. A romantic movie without sex and lust?

**Score:** 34
**Body:** Okay so I'm so fucking tired of watching romantic movies with sex in them, no matter if the movie is a small indie production or a grand blockbuster, an old classic or a new one, about heterosexual couples or homosexual ones, directed or written by a man or a woman, why on earth do they all have to insert sex and lust into it, can't people make movies about love WITHOUT being horny? The best movie in this regard i have watched is LA LA LAND although the ending didn't really worked for me, but atleast it does not get horny.

Thanks for reading my rant, as you can see from this, that i want a romantic drama or romantic comedy which does not get sexual AT ALL, i want it to be totally about love.

Thank you!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. If you haven’t seen Pride & Prejudice (2005), I’d start there.
  - This was my first thought as well. And really, any of Jane Austen’s adaptations fit the bill, with the exception of maybe Sanditon. 
2. Princess Bride
3. Loads of classics:

Casablanca

Brief Encounter

Sabrina

The African Queen
4. I kind of want to say "In The Mood For Love" because it - to me - feels genuinely about the real chemistry and tension, rather than some base lusty hornyness.

But I'm just not sure if it's bordering on too horny for you. Open to thoughts from others here?
5. While You Were Sleeping
6. The Wedding Singer

50 First Dates
7. The before triology
8. You've Got Mail, Sleepless in Seattle, While You Were Sleeping, The Vow, The Family Man, The Wedding Planner, As Good as it Gets.
9. Roman Holiday

## 3. Looking for a happy escape

**Score:** 29
**Body:** My friend is dealing with chemo, and is looking for films to take her away from the stress and anxiety of real life. Something funny, uplifting, or beautiful would be appreciated. I’d appreciate any suggestions! 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Princess Bride is my go to. I also love Dungeons and Dragons: Honor Among Thieves 
2. Nacho Libre
3. Amelie 
4. Maybe some Christopher Guest movies:  Waiting for Guffman, A Mighty Wind or Best In Show. Funny and feel good. 

A recent-ish good comedy: Theater Camp
  - I came to recommend Best in Show. That movie is a hit with every single person I’ve ever showed it to..like, pure delight!
5. Eurotrip (light nudity)- Teen comedy based in the 2000's about online dating and the lengths a college bound student will go.

The Hangover (light nudity)

The Incredibles

Wreck it Ralph

Down Periscope or Sgt. Bilko (military comedy movies from the 90's)

Loaded Weapon/The Naked Gun/Airplane (some nudity) (original spoof/comedy movies)

Tommy Boy

Coming 2 America (some nudity)

Scott Pilgrim vs The World (hilarious comedy with a nerdy flex)

Ready Player One (a complete nerdy fun story about balancing life between the virtual and real world)

The Mask

Dumb & Dumber

Ace Ventura: Pet Detective

Fast Times at Ridgemont High

Ferris Buellers Day Off
6. Ponyo (2008)
7. Crazy Stupid Love
8. My husband went through cancer treatments and we would lay in the hospital with an iPad and watch episodes of Vice Principals and cry of laughter!
9. Howls Moving Castle 

Smokey and the Bandit

Cannonball Run

Top Secret 

Any Mel Brooks movie

## 4. GOAT Children's movies for a new parent

**Score:** 20
**Body:** just had my first son and im into collecting physical media. I don't have that many childrens movies at the moment but I want to get some for him so when the time comes I have them..what are the essentials?? 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Toy Story

Iron Giant
2. Fantastic Mr. Fox
3. The Wild Robot

Edit - The Lorax (and most Dr. Seuss)
  - I think this one might be my favorite “kids” movie, it hits especially hard as a parent 
4. Studio Ghibli... My neighbor Totoro and Ponyo are my kids favorites. 
5. WALL-E
6. All of Disney, and most Pixar


The Wizard of Oz


Star Wars original trilogy 


Time Bandits 


As much Three Stooges as you can find


7. Brave Little Toaster
8. Babe and Babe: Pig In the City
9. How to Train Your Dragon

## 5. Women directors in experimental, dark art house film

**Score:** 14
**Body:** Hi, i came to this realization that alot of my favorite films are made by men, some of them being Enter the Void- gasper noe, Kneecap- rich peppiat, Trainspotting- Danny dole. One of my favorite films by female directors are Lost in Translation- Sofia Copella and The Strangers in Good Company- Cynthia scoot. They all definitely have similar underlying themes, I honestly don’t know exactly the genre these all fall into but you get the basic understanding. I’m having a hard time finding more female directors in this category of film and cinematography. I would love any suggestions that aren’t the main ones everyone knows. I want to get deeper into film and finding women directors is very important for me. Any suggestions would be greatly appreciated!!! 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Claire Denis — Trouble Every Day (2001) is the one, horror register but formally rigorous, Vincent Gallo and Béatrice Dalle, sex and cannibalism and Paris shot like a fever dream, very much that Enter the Void visceral mood without the first-person gimmick. Beau Travail (1999) if you want something slower, Foreign Legion in Djibouti, barely any dialogue, just bodies and landscape and repressed desire building to one of the best endings in cinema.

Lucile Hadžihalilović — Evolution (2015), island of boys and nurses, no explanation for what's happening medically or mythologically, just this sterile body-horror unease, underwater cinematography, unsettling the whole way through. Innocence (2004) is the earlier one, girls in a forest school, same fairy-tale-but-wrong register.

Lynne Ramsay — We Need to Talk About Kevin (2011) matches that Trainspotting fractured-editing thing, Tilda Swinton processing her son's violence in non-linear fragments, red everywhere, very much about dread and guilt as formal structure. You Were Never Really Here (2017) has Joaquin Phoenix as a PTSD hitman, same elliptical editing, trauma told through what the film refuses to show you.

Julia Ducournau — Raw (2016) if you want body horror, vet school cannibal awakening, Titane (2021) is the weirder one, woman fucks a car and goes on the run, both are aggressive and strange in ways that feel closer to Gaspar Noé's register than most arthouse.
2. Kelly Reichardt, start with Wendy and Lucy 

Katherine Bigelow is very good, but not much fits the vibe of the movies you listed. Maybe Near Dark? 

Jane Schoenbrun might work. We're all Going to the World's Fair and I Saw the TV Glow 

The cinematographer for Sinners, Autumn Arkapaw, was the first woman to win the Oscar, which is pretty cool.
3. Agnes Varda- Faces Places. For experimental .
Coralie Fargeat- Revenge. For darker.
4. The Lost Daughter directed by Maggie Gyllenhaal

Aftersun directed by Charlotte Wells - I highly recommend 


5. You might like stuff from Julia Ducournau (Raw, Titane) and Lynne Ramsay (You Were Never Really Here).

Also maybe check out Claire Denis — some of her films have that same unsettling / atmospheric vibe.

It’s a bit harder to find female directors in that exact niche but they’re definitely out there.
6. Jennifer’s Body
7. The Piano - Jane Campion

The Hitchhiker - Ida Lupino

But, the one I would suggest over all others is a Russian director.  
It’s a difficult film to watch (not violent, or gory) but it’s because of the story and the amazing performances the director gets from her actors. It has one of the most difficult scenes I’ve ever watched on film. Heartbreakingly beautiful, but a beautiful film, nonetheless. 10/10

The Ascent (‘77) - Larisa Shepitko 
8. ...I hated Choke.


...but I loved The Mastermind
9. - Kajillionaire 
- Titane 
- High Life
- La Chimera 
- Raw
- Woodshock 
- The Love Witch
- Daises 

10. Lynne Ramsay is my goat

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Christina Hendricks turns 51 today. Happy birthday to her!

**Score:** 824

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Thought she was great in Firefly but absolutely loved her in Mad Men.

![gif](giphy|Ikqov3ATbAtTa)
  - ![gif](giphy|pxejTFJRjLqw0)
    - Single-handedly brought back the smart, sexy full-figured bombshell, imo. *Vavoom! 🚀*
2. Despite her limited role, she was solid in Drive. The motel scene gave me the biggest shock.
3. MOMMY 
  - ![gif](giphy|8mkykXIicXOVms2xYE)
  - God yes!
4. The special hell
5. That dress makes it look like she has one huge boob on the right.
6. She's only 3 years older than me.  Happy Birthday gorgeous.

## 2. Christopher Walken was an absolute delight in Seven Psychopaths. Martin McDonagh delivered a really entertaining film. What do you think about Seven Psychopaths?

**Score:** 815

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I loved it. I watched it after In Bruges, and I wanted more of the playful, fairly light but decently grounded comedy. Walken was brilliant as usual, but really most of the cast nailed the vibe as well.

I didn't look up anything about the movie, because I wanted to go in blind, and watched Banshees of Inisherin next.  NOT what I signed up for. Amazing, but ...... wow.
2. The cemetery is Sam Rockwell's best single scene of his entire career.
  - Did you enjoy good luck have fun don’t die? I seem to like anything he’s in
    - Love Sam Rockwell in anything as well.  My ONLY complaint about good luck have fun don't die.  is that I didn't get a Rockwell dance.  I really think he could have easily worked in a little dance number. 
3. It is one of my favorite movies.  I watched it so much that my wife made me stop watching it (fair).  It's also dark enough that if she catches me watching it, she gets angry.  
4. I thought it was really good, love the dialogue and the chemistry between the lead characters.
5. one great thing about this movie is that I would completely forget about it and can enjoy it again
6. Loved this one even more it's made by the same guys:

https://preview.redd.it/6zfokdt2lwyg1.png?width=671&format=png&auto=webp&s=1eb56727f2fb8de43c62bcbe05bcbe692ca38fb5


7. You can say its meta without sounding really pretentious 
8. possibly the most star studded movie this century to ever fade out of collective consciousness in less than a year...

## 3. Serious question: What’s the appeal of Timothée Chalamet & why does he keep getting cast in top roles?

**Score:** 684

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. See *Ladybird*, *Call Me By Your Name*, and *Little Women*. He was fantastic at a young age, which gives you an enormous leg up to be a star by the time you’re older. He’s not a chameleon really but he has believable intensity.

EDIT: *Beautiful Boy* is also a film.
  - The King is rarely mentioned. I really liked that movie. 
  - \> He’s not a chameleon…

True, because it’s actually pronounced Chalameteon.
  - I liked him The King and he was good in Dune.
2. Was genuinely good in all the artsy movies that he did. Then Dune 2 comes out where his character was the modern day Anakin but with way better acting. His performance in Marty Supreme was like his version of Leo in WoWS.

The dude has range and chooses good scripts. He deserves the top roles.
  - Marty Supreme reminded me of Uncut Gems with how he’s just nonstop hustling 
    - Same director brother 
3. https://preview.redd.it/579e6qnoauyg1.jpeg?width=620&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=80ce8d134a9066a12b423e2471d27a45196740f0
4. I thought he pulled off the leading role in his most recent big movies including Dune, A Complete Unknown and Marty Supreme. Hence the award nominations in recent years.

He’s a very good actor, and both his roles as Marty Supreme and Bob Dylan shows he can play at times obnoxious characters but bring some sympathy to the roles.

I actually think he’s underrated as an actor, and I think his personal life has impacted that  
5. He’s a very good actor willing to take risks, and he has a lot of appeal for youngsters, who are heavily courted by studios. 

## 4. Thoughts on Andre Braugher’s acting?

**Score:** 445
**Body:** I deleted the other Andre Braugher post because I figured he was more successful in television than in films even though he stole scenes in a couple of films including glory. So I decided to ask about what did you think about his acting overall. Great actor gone too soon

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. He is absolutely brilliant as Captain Holt. I could rewatch B99 a million times and laugh 
  - Before he became a captain in NY he was a homicide detective in Bmore. 


He is really great in Homicide: Life in the Street. "three men and Adina" could be a short film on it's own, imo . 
    - Homicide was a exceptional show!
2. His absolute stand out role is Detective Frank Pembleton from Homicide Life on the Street. He should have won so many Emmys from that role. 
3. He has a penchant for heavy breasted women.
  - There is nothing better than a pair of big, weighty breasts.
4. He's genuinely a great actor and an excellent example of the blurred lines between television and film these days. Decades ago, film was the 'promotion' from TV and some people still harbor that attitude. But it has shifted pretty radically over the years. I'd put Braugher's television acting up there with a good chunk of the great film actors.

His character in Brooklyn 99 is hilarious and brilliant, and his acting *for* that character is flawless. Captain Holt starts out as a soulless robot but towards the end has a genuine warm spot for a select few things (Cheddar, his dog, for one). And shows in the Wuntch episodes that he is in fact on the verge of derangement. I mean seriously, how good do you have to be to play "Robotic, emotionless machine of a police captain with extraordinary range."




5. ![gif](giphy|3oxRmES1DuIB9tcBR6)
6. Men of a certain age is a very underrated show, that was my first experience with him outside of some random cameos.

Brooklyn 99 is probably on my top 10 of tv comedies and without him it wouldn't even be half of what it was.

He's one of the few comedic actors IMO who can turn on a dime from saying something profoundly funny and then make you choke up with real raw emotion 
7. Captain holt is a top tier character

![gif](giphy|f3jZ8moRBbEvNJjOtu)

## 5. Mad Max: Fury Road Crowned Best Action Movie Ever by Collider

**Score:** 362

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I just saw Fury Road again last night on the big screen at my local arthouse theatre as part of their 52nd Anniversary screenings

It’s absolutely one of the absolute best action ever made imo too

Non-stop motor mayhem
  - I saw it in a whim in a theater...and then went back 3 more times.
2. Terminator 2 , Aliens and Die Hard would like to have a chat.

Post 2000s I would agree but if you take the absolute treasure trove of action flicks that is the 80's and 90's Including James Cameron at his absolute peak .... No not by a mile.
3. It’s up there for sure 
4. Collider should watch more Arnold Schwarzenegger movies
5. It's up there, top tier for sure. What about Terminator 1, Terminator 2, Die Hard, Rambo, Aliens, Predator. I think T2 has the best claim to the top spot. Fury Road might be the most visually stunning action film.
6. I mean.. the greatest EVER? I can't agree with that, but it was REALLY good. I had a great time watching it. 
7. Oh what a lovely day! 
8. It was okay but not remotely close to best ever.
9. I'd add Drunken Master II to that list as well.

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Curious about the history behind how popcorn bevame the standard movie snack in theatres.

**Score:** 87
**Body:** How did popcorn, out of all the snacks that existed back then, turn out to be THAT standard snacks at every movie place?

It's become such a staple of an item that a movie theatre always makes sure popcorn is made available even if none of the other snacks machines are working. 

And it wouldn't have been such a thing that'd spark my curiosity if it was a regional thing. Nope, popcorn is a cultural element for movies rather than a munch ALL AROUND THE WORLD.

What's the history?

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. it's because it is extremely cheap, high profit margin, and you can make volumes of it to feed a lot of people all at once. this is why movie theatres are really just popcorn dispensaries.

popcorn was also the favoured snack for entertainment pre-movies, too, going back to circuses. although peanuts are also common there. 
  - Popcorn is also easy for traveling circus as the corn is small, yet the volume increases substantially 
    - yeah exactly. 
  - Also when I was a kid cinema in my area of UK also did Candy floss which sort of backs up the circus fair theory.

I’d totally forgot about Candy floss at my local cinema.

Perhaps another question is why did peanuts and candy floss dies out?

    - honestly i think popcorn won just due to its sheer profitability, and the fact that it 'keeps' all day (unlike cotton candy) and is served hot, which gives it a feeling of luxury (lol) compared to peanuts, which seem more like street food by comparison.
    - Peanuts = shells = mess

Also, peanuts are way more $$ than popcorn. 
2. It was at circuses and fairs in the mid 19th century and then when those started including Nickelodeons and early movies it was at times banned, as the movies were silent and popcorn too noisy. When talkies movies arrived it was let back in so to speak. It’s cheap and easy to make a profit on and it’s delicious. Pretty much that’s all it was 
  - The real fun fact here is that nickelodeon isn't just a kids TV channel.
3. History.com: [When Movie Theaters Tried to Ban Popcorn](https://www.history.com/articles/why-popcorn-is-movie-snack)

TL;DR: Popcorn and peanuts were already popular fair/amusement park foods, but early cinemas banned them for the noise and mess they made in their 'higher-class' establishments. Patrons would still attempt to sneak them in from street vendors. Only with the advent of talkies and the Depression, when cinemas became the predominant entertainment venue, did theatres allow some of those street vendors into their lobbies. Soon, they started popping their own popcorn. The rationale for popcorn? 97-98% gross profit margins. Corn kernels, salt, and margarine are cheap.
4. In addition to the profit margin, is also worth noting the space and time needed for prep.

Those big popcorn poppers you see in theaters have been around longer than moving pictures.  You can make a huge quantity of food, in a short amount of time, in a 3x3 foot corner of the room, with almost no effort.



## 2. watched the mysterious skin(2004)

**Score:** 42
**Body:** I bet this movie gets talked about a lot, and I dont have anything new to say, but what a despair inducing movie. The straight version of this movie is requiem for a dream, but dont think that movie comes close here. Ive never felt as though I was particularly helpless as an audience member outside of this experience. Also surprising to find something so utterly lacking in tropes, no adjudication for the coach or anything like that. Not really any kind of true "moment of understanding" for Brian or Neil either, even in the last scene all they can do is rehearse the events of their abuse, also the music is 10/10. 

### Top 2 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. It’s interesting that you make the comparison to requiem for a dream, I don’t really see that at all. Requiem feels overly stylized snd sensationalist, I get the feeling aronofsky probably doesnt have any experience with the subject matter he’s dealing with. Mysterious skin feels the opposite. It’s very grounded. In the most horrific moments of the film from what I remember the stylistic flourishes are stripped away snd you’re only left with the cold reality of the violence/trauma. I also just think the characters are so damn flushed out. I think that’s what makes mysterious skin so brilliant and so awful to watch, it’s just so damn real. 
  - yea youre probably right i dont think they share a lot of cinematic dna. but i think there is something there in the sense of watching neil make "bad" decisions, i think the sense of helplessness as a viewer is there in both movies and I think thats neat 
2. I think what makes *Mysterious Skin* hit so hard is exactly what you pointed out—it refuses to give the audience any kind of emotional ‘release.’ There’s no neat arc or catharsis, just the reality of traumma persisting,  It almost feels like the film is intentionally denying the viewer the comfort that most narratives provide.

## 3. Watched Manchester by the Sea today.

**Score:** 35
**Body:** It was probably the only movie where the grief is so realistically handled. Here's my insight.

1. I felt that those flashbacks are literally Lee's thoughts, we were in his mind during the flashbacks. That's why they felt that sudden, inconsistent and jarring.

2. The screenplay is so mundane, it is totally everyday conversations, nothing special, nothing over the top, nothing difficult. 

This is something the hardest to write because to convey grief in such everyday conversations, you actually have to face it, otherwise you might take some help from long monologues or lonely nights with crying eyes. I don't think no one in that team would really have had the pain of Lee Chandler and still to write such an amazing character is mind-blowing.

3. Casey Affleck was so amazing, his grief showed through his behavior, yet he kept calm mostly and never let it out himself. He never showed his grief, he never sobbed, except once, never screamed, he kept everything to himself.

We were screaming for Lee to empty his heart out and not let everything to himself, the movie didn't even offer a moment of it. That river didn't flow. The ending was so anti-cathartic, which makes this movie more unforgettable.

### Top 4 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I love how the funny scenes worked. They didnt feel force or separeted to the bigger story but very well implemented. And helped to see how the relationship grew between Casey Affleck and the boy.
  - That's something I loved about the movie. It's everyday life. It can be funny, sad, exciting or boring. The movie shines because it is grounded, and everyone having some kind of grief would immediately relate to it.
2. That scene in the street everyone remembers....I have heard how stressed Affleck and Williams were about the day circled on the calendar for it, but they chased down every "um" and "uh" and delivered the emotions beautifully. Great performances all through. 

Lonergan is such a great writer. Besides all the great naturalistic dialogue, he gets metaphors into his screenplay that are very elegant and don't announce themselves too loudly. All the talk about the ground being hard and cold has a practical application (the delay in burial) but also reflects Lee's state: too impenetrable to allow anything new in. The specific cause of Joe's death, "chronic heart failure," applies to his surviving brother in a non-medical sense, and is echoed in Randi's line: "My heart was broken. It's always gonna be broken. And I know yours is broken too." 

The Academy picked the right Original Screenplay winner that year. 

I do find notes of hope in the way the story works out, but it's an improvement in degrees not close to the usual Hollywood 180 in stories like this. It's a truthful movie. 
3. I've only watched the movie one time - I can't muster the will to do a rewatch - but I feeling a hint that Casey Affleck's character was a bit better off mentally at the end of the movie than he was at the start. May have been wishful thinking on my part.

It's an amazing performance and movie .. that I don't think I'll ever watch again.
  - No, you're right, he was. It's subtle. When he's fixing the guy's water heater near the end, we can see he's a little more alive and engaged than he was with the tenants at his job at the beginning. Then it's clear in the ball-bouncing scene that he and Patrick are going to stay connected and support each other, and we see them fishing. 
    - He even says he’s getting an apartment with extra room for when Patrick visits.

## 4. What Have You Been Watching? (Week of (May 03, 2026)

**Score:** 10
**Body:**  Please don't downvote opinions. Only downvote comments that don't contribute anything. Check out the [WHYBW archives](https://www.reddit.com/r/TrueFilm/wiki/index#wiki_what_have_you_been_watching.3F).

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. **Raptus (2025)** fun canadian low-budget sex-bot horror thriller that puts some real emphasis on making the male sex bot stuff actually horny. Really liked this, but it seems not many people do haha

**Rose (2026)** period piece gender shenanigans with Sandra Hüller as a woman pretending to be a man during the 17th century in Germany so she can inherit a farm. beautifully shot in black and white! highly recommend this, though if you want to come out of a movie happy, this isn't it. Had some overlap stylistically with The Passion of Joan of Arc (1928), or so I felt like. Highly recommended! Looked great in the cinema!

**Climax (2018)** Baby's first Gaspar Noe (it's me; I'm baby). Caught this as part of a horror throwback screening and it was really quite a banger for about half its run time. You can however feel that many of these people on screen aren't trained in acting. Goes hard with a proper sound system though.

**The Blood On Satan's Claw (1971)** this was beautifullllll on the big screen. the vibes in this, especially from everything shot outdoors, are immaculate. one of the most impactful rape scenes I've ever seen, it's really good. (also: didn't think anything this month would be able to top this as far as general rape-y-ness goes, but alas--I did watch Raptor after this and that's just next level rapey. So be advised and all that.)

**The Devil Wears Prada 2 (2026)** watchable as far as legacy sequels go, though I found it far more depressing than the OG and like... story-wise it's just not as engaging. Had a hard time feeling bad for Andy at any point in this. And the backstabbing etc was very forced. But other than that it does a few fun things.

**Michael (2026)** some of the worst CGI animals and concert backgrounds I've seen in a long time in this film. musical sequences were overall quite fun, if uninspired in exactly the way those in the Bohemian Rhapsody movie were. Super strange decision to give Latoya like three lines and then not have Janet at all, but whatever. Would have been far more interesting if it was shot from ANY other POV than Michael Jackson's imho. 
2. *The Drama*

Really enjoyed it. Could have used a bit of a trim in the first act but genuinely hilarious in places. One person slightly miscast in a support role. I’m a little confused about people’s horror and revulsion on The Big Secret. I would find something like that tragic rather than a reason to attack the person making the admission. But maybe it’s an American thing, I’m not too sure. 
  - > But maybe it’s an American thing, I’m not too sure.

that was my takeaway as well haha

nobody in my cinema was particular shocked or saw the issue as dramatic, really.
3. **The Phoenician Scheme (2025, dir. Wes Anderson)**

After suspending himself in a metatextual soup for two movies, Anderson returns to Earth and tells a more explicitly political story. The Phoenician Scheme tells a story about the geopolitical ramifications of unrestrained capitalism with a lot of nuance and visual flair. Zsa-Zsa Korda is probably the most unlikeable protagonist in Anderson's catalog, and while the film doesn't make any excuses for him and his behavior, it's hard to come away from The Phoenician Scheme totally hating him. His personality is extensively developed, and his arc is well-realized through his interactions with his daughter and his spiritual introspections. The film recognizes that while ideological shifts are beyond the scope of one person, individual change still has to come from within. Korda doesn't end the film as a good person, but a lot of progress has been made, and redemption is ongoing.

Attending Korda and his daughter are wonderful side characters played by Michael Cera, Jeffrey Wright, Riz Ahmed, Benedict Cumberbatch, Tom Hanks, Bryan Cranston, and surprise guest Richard Ayaode. Dean Lerner has sure come a long way. Wonderfully quirky performances are to be expected from Anderson, as well as phenomenal production design and cinematography. I think at the end of the day The Phoenician Scheme is standard Anderson, which is pretty good in a vacuum, but I think I'll have to rewatch it to properly love it.

**Bottle Rocket (1996, dir. Wes Anderson)**

Anderson's first feature is brimming with many narrative hallmarks of his later career. While Bottle Rocket doesn't look like a dollhouse or switch aspect ratios every other scene, it tells a complex emotional story about young people and their struggle to find their place in the world. The Wilson brothers are both excellent and have great rapport. It's easy to relate to the story and the characters, and the oddities of their experiences only elevate the emotion. It's like a less batshit insane version of Wild at Heart. Anderson displays commendable confidence, and it's easy to see how he rocketed to Oscar-adjacent status half a decade and three films later.

**Resident Evil: Welcome to Raccoon City (2021, dir. Johannes Roberts)**

This is the first, arguably maybe second, live-action Resident Evil film to actively attempt to adapt one of the video games. In fact, it adapts two of them at once, which in concept might sound effective, but I think this is the crux of the film's problems. There's too much plot and too many characters in this movie, and nothing is developed to a satisfying extent. Character motivations, plot beats, and even entire characters are confused and lost in the shuffle. On top of that, the tone is a mess and the film is seldom ever scary. Roberts can't build tension effectively, and can't even pull off the simplest jumpscares. Welcome to Raccoon City is an improvement over the Paul WS Anderson series, but it doesn't really excel in doing anything interesting or fun.

**Shoes (1916, dir. Lois Weber)**

There are many movies from the 1910s about people's struggles with poverty, and it's easy for Shoes to get lost in the shuffle. However, it's elevated by Weber's direction. She has a strong attention to detail, fleshing out the characters' lives to a realistic extent. The decision to ground the story in Eva's desire to buy a pair of shoes goes a long way in forging a connection between the audience and the character. Through expert cinematography and exceptional acting, Weber and actress Mary McLaren make sure you know just how bad Eva's footwear situation is. It's poetic synecdoche, a true application of less is more. Any film can show a family living in extreme squalor, but I don't know if I could feel more bad for them than I do Eva when I see her dragging her worn-out shoes through a rainy street. What also helps is that Eva is a better developed character than most in films of this kind. She's sacrificed so much for the welfare of her family, and all she asks for is a pair of new shoes so she isn't burdened with extreme pain every time she goes to work. There's virtually no levity in her journey, even by the end. 1916 movies with unhappy endings weren't very common, and Shoes' is powerful and provocative. The same can be said for the rest of the movie, really.
4. **To Kill a Mockingbird (1962, Robert Mulligan) — re-watch** — My opinion on this hasn’t changed much since I last reviewed it. Still an **8/10**.

**Like Father, Like Son (2013, Hirokazu Kore-eda)** — Feel like I wanted to like this one more than I did. It's not a bad movie, but it kind of feels to me like it was made according to a template for a slow-burn prestige drama. Even though it’s not a template that I’m a great fan of, I do still think I respect the craftsmanship of this one enough to give it a modest recommendation. **7/10**

**The Departed (2006, Martin Scorsese) — re-watch** — Alright, I think I over-corrected in my last review of this movie. Yes, *The Departed* is, in a number of ways, a better-made movie than the original *Infernal Affairs*, but I think I went too far in talking as if the loss of the original’s existential themes did not weaken it at all. Remaking an existential drama as a crowd-pleasing action movie is one thing, but even a good crowd-pleasing action movie should still find *some* emotional hook that gives a reason for the audience to care what happens. *The Departed* just replaces the deeper emotions of *Infernal Affairs* with more violence and swearing and hallmark Scorsese tricks. And it’s all very well-shot and well-edited and well-acted, probably more so than in the original, but is it about anything? Does any of it feel like it matters? Not really. Lowering this to a **7/10**.

**Boyfriends and Girlfriends (1987, Éric Rohmer)** — Possibly my favourite of the Rohmer movies I’ve seen so far… which isn’t saying a lot. **7/10**

**Say Anything… (1989, Cameron Crowe)** — Been trying to put my finger on why I liked this so much more than any of the other classic ‘80s teen comedies I’ve seen. I think it’s because Crowe strikes a rare balance between empathizing with his teenage protagonists and their feelings that their relationship matters are life-and-death, while also being self-aware about the fact that these are things that, in the grand scheme of things, will one day seem trivial. One never gets the sense here — as one often does with, for instance, John Hughes — that Crowe is pretending to think teenagers really *do* have everything figured out. **10/10**

**Movie of the week:** *Say Anything*

It’s getting too difficult to participate here, with very little feedback and returns.

## 5. Best Movie of the year so far- Blue Heron

**Score:** 6
**Body:** I saw three movies yesterday, all about different forms of art and our relationship to its creation.

*The Christophers* is maybe my favorite Soderbergh in a decade. It's small (like most of his movie have been lately), tight, and slightly sterile. The two leads are contrasts in age, race, gender, and character. One of them is a youngish black woman who's planning to forge the works of the older white man. I like movies about crime and lies and art, and this one works to ask the question: What does it take to make a great work of art? And why can't we always do it?

*Mother Mary* has the same actress as *The Christophers*: Michael Coel. Again it's a movie centering on two people. This time she's paired with Anne Hathaway. It's kind of a horror movie. It's about relationships necessary to create art, and about the damage you can't undo, which *The Christophers* is slightly about too (all 3 movies are a bit about the pain of losing someone). It's gorgeous. I'm a big David Lowery fan, and I'm still trying to wrap my heads around this. It shows art as collaboration, and it's about the power of female friendship. It reminds me of Zadie Smith's *Swing Time*, which I've never read.

*Blue Heron*. Best movie of the day and the of the year. It follows a young girl and her family as they deal with her trouble older brother. Then there's a jump and a turn and not quite a twist, and it becomes about memory and loss and the value of filmmaking. There are gorgeous shots and beautiful speeches. It brings to mind *The Souvenir* and *Aftersun*. It made me weep, and I think more people should watch it.

I'm curious if people think more indie films are about art and artmaking than they used to be. Obviously that's always been a big theme, but is it even more popular now?

### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Blue heron is my second favorite movie of the year so far and Nirvanna the Band the Show the Movie is my #1. Makes me proud to see 2 small budget Canadian films topping my list.

I haven't seen Mother Mary yet (planning to watch it next week), but I've seen the Christophers and for whatever reason felt super disconnected to it. I think its a pretty decently made movie in most aspects and I thought that there were some themes that gave me something to chew on, but for whatever reason I just did not enjoy my experience with the movie. I actually think it's my least favorite movie I have seen so far this year, but I also have no seen a single bad movie so far this year so I'm not saying that it's a bad film.

Obviously I liked *The Christophers*, but I totally get not liking it...There's something about Soderbergh's style lately that feels too cold. Like he's studying something or examining it rather than just putting it all there. I just liked what was being examined this time
